<par>Paang is actually <emph>Paangkhua</emph> (Chin), Check lg name list if <emph>Paang</emph> is valid alternate name.</par>
<par>Note the final <latinform>-t</latinform>; check form in Zaiwa jianzhi.</par>
<par>Why isn’ t this form <reconstruction>*šit</reconstruction>? Check Burling “PLB” .</par>
<par>The macron is here meant to indicate mid-level tone 33 (< *1); not as in JAM’ s Lahu transcription where it would indicate low-level tone 11 (< *2).\-</par>

<par>The <latinform>tl-</latinform> cluster in this Miju form looks like a reduction of the disyllabic sequence <latinform>tɯ-lai</latinform> (cf. <latinform>pɑ³¹ tɯ³¹ lɑi⁵⁵</latinform> 'center'). This disyllabic form in turn is susceptible of two interpretations. Either it is a two-morpheme sequence where the first element is < <reconstruction>*du</reconstruction> NAVEL/UMBILICAL CORD (i.e. < <reconstruction>*du-laːy</reconstruction>; see below), or more plausibly it, as well as the first consonant in the monosyllabic form <latinform>tlái</latinform>, is simply a reflex of the <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> prefix (<reconstruction>*s-laːy</reconstruction> > <latinform>tlái</latinform>, or <reconstruction>*s-laːy</reconstruction> > <latinform>tɯ-lai</latinform>). See also the Bantawa, Kham, and Lakher (Mara) reflexes with <latinform>tl-</latinform> in this section, as well as the WT form with <latinform>lt-</latinform>, under <xref ref="519">#519</xref>.</par>

<par>The <latinform>tl-</latinform> cluster in this Miju form looks like a reduction of the disyllabic sequence <latinform>tɯ-lai</latinform> (cf. <latinform>pɑ³¹ tɯ³¹ lɑi⁵⁵</latinform> 'center').</par>

<par>The <latinform>tl-</latinform> in the last syllable seems to be the reflex of the <reconstruction>*s-l</reconstruction> cluster (cf. the Lakher [Mara] and Miju forms with similar initials). If the second syllable <latinform>-bu(y)-</latinform> were really just a fully vocalized reflex of a labial prefix <reconstruction>*bV-</reconstruction>, this form would reflect a doubly-prefixed prototype like <reconstruction>*b-s-lay</reconstruction> (cf. the Ashö Sandoway <latinform>hmlai</latinform> < <reconstruction>*s-m-lay</reconstruction>). On the other hand, the second syllable seems to descend from a separate etymon (perhaps <xref ref="164">#164</xref> or <xref ref="2111">#2111</xref>), especially in view of Kham forms like <latinform>pũ:h-tali</latinform>.</par>
<par>For Bantawa <latinform>tl-</latinform>, cf. Lakher [Mara] and Miju forms with similar reflexes of <xref ref="517">#517</xref>.</par>
<par><latinform>¹šə¹gri. </latinform>M1: 1390 (on basis of previous) or just p?</par>
<par>This word is not to be found in the dictionaries of Judson 1893/1953/1966 or Bernot 1978-92.</par>

<par>This is evidently a loan into Lahu; the high falling tone is anomalous, since it does not occur after /h/ in native words. Furthermore, the rhyme <latinform>*-ya</latinform> should yield Lahu <latinform>-ɛ</latinform>.</par>
<par>My Ao consultants say this means 'sky', and that no one they know is familiar with the alleged 'year' meaning. Even Clark 1911 has nothing of the sort.</par>

<par>Liangmei <latinform>ma-rui</latinform>, Rengma <latinform>¹tʌ¹ro</latinform>, Rongmei <latinform>n-rûai</latinform>, Zemei <latinform>¹n⁵rui</latinform> prob. all mean FOWL (check Weidert).</par>
<par>u is a body-part prefix in Angami</par>

<par>The vowel of Angami <latinform>²mie</latinform> seems an unlikely reflex of <reconstruction>*-o</reconstruction> (< <xref ref="1353">#1353</xref>); yet the alternative of assigning this form to <xref ref="547">#547</xref> seems no better, since the regular Angami reflex of <reconstruction>*-ak</reconstruction> seems to be <latinform>-o</latinform> (e.g. WEAVE <reconstruction>*dak</reconstruction> > Ang. <latinform>do</latinform>; ANT <reconstruction>*-rwak</reconstruction> > Ang. <latinform>cho</latinform>).</par>
<par>There is some difficulty in interpreting these Naga forms from Weidert, since they are glossed literally as "one's wing".</par>
<par>This is a problematic record. Listed under “(North Assam)” , it's not clear if it's meant to be a protoform or if the language name was left out inadvertently. See AW-TBT set 690.</par>
<par>This form with initial <latinform>b-</latinform> might be a loan from Tibetan, since all the other Tamangic forms have dental or retroflex initials.</par>
<par>marked for deletion in Blue notebook. Why?</par>
<par>The Karenish forms for HAIR/FUR/FEATHER/BEARD, etc. are confusing.</par>
<par>These Bodo-Garo forms illustrate a reduction of the lateral initial to zero.</par>
